reasoning: The informal statement asks to prove that L(a) = {x ∈ R | xa = 0} is an ideal of R. The Lean statement asserts the existence of an ideal I whose underlying set equals {x : R | x*a = 0}. Via the SetLike coercion from Ideal R to Set R, this is precisely the standard way to say "the set {x | xa = 0} is an ideal": a set is an ideal iff it is equal (as a set) to the underlying set of some Ideal R. The quantifier structure (for a given a in a commutative ring R) matches, and no extra hypotheses are introduced or dropped. Hence the Lean signature is logically equivalent to the informal claim.
